title: Generalization of a localized-state formation mechanism in finite lattices with interaction nonlinearity
abstract: We study how time-periodic, spatially localized states are born from the linear spectrum of a \emph{finite} lattice as the nonlinearity is switched on. In earlier work we treated this question for a diatomic chain with on-site nonlinearity and developed a framework that continues a near-edge linear mode in amplitude and controls the resulting perturbation series uniformly in the chain length. The present paper shows that the same framework applies to the more difficult case of Fermi--Pasta--Ulam--Tsingou (FPUT) interaction nonlinearity. The key is a structural relation between the FPUT and on-site nonlinearities, which allows the estimates obtained in the on-site setting to be transferred to the FPUT setting. As before, the analysis yields a quantitative radius of convergence, $\eps=Θ(1/\sqrt{n})$ for a chain of length $2n$, below which the near-edge mode stays extended and above which the orbit localizes and its frequency leaves the band. The diatomic chain is used only as a test case; both the formation mechanism and the method are model-independent and are expected to extend to other short-range nonlinearities and to higher dimensions.
summary: This theoretical paper analyzes the transition from extended linear modes to localized states in finite lattices subjected to cubic FPUT interaction nonlinearity. By developing a general framework, the authors successfully extend prior methods to this complex nonlinear case. The key finding is the determination of a quantitative convergence radius $\epsilon = \Theta(1/\sqrt{n})$, providing deep insight into the stability and localization scale of these wave solutions.
main_problem: The paper investigates the mechanism by which time-periodic, spatially localized states form from an extended linear mode near a band edge when a nonlinearity is gradually introduced.
main_result: It establishes that this formation process can be quantitatively analyzed for the FPUT interaction nonlinearity, yielding a convergence radius $\epsilon = \Theta(1/\sqrt{n})$ below which the mode remains extended.
method: The analysis employs perturbation theory combined with Lyapunov–Schmidt reduction to continue an orbit from the linear spectrum into the nonlinear regime.
model_or_system: The system is a finite one-dimensional diatomic lattice governed by equations of motion involving both linear spring forces and cubic FPUT interaction nonlinearity. The method is claimed to be model-independent for other short-range nonlinearities and higher dimensions.
key_observables: Radius of convergence ($\epsilon$), frequency shift ($\omega_{<2>}$), and the profile concentration (middle vs. edge localization).
important_parameters: Chain length ($2n$), nonlinearity strength, and spectral gaps which scale as $\Theta(1/n^2)$ for finite lattices.
main_assumptions: The analysis is restricted to the diatomic chain as a test case; it assumes specific structural relations between different nonlinearities (e.g., FPUT vs. on-site).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate schematic representations of the finite FPUT-type diatomic chain, showing the evolution of periodic orbits from extended states towards central or boundary concentrations.
paper_structure: The paper builds by first establishing a framework transferable from simple on-site nonlinearities to complex FPUT interactions. It then uses spectral analysis and perturbation theory (Lyapunov–Schmidt reduction) to calculate convergence radii, culminating in inductive estimates that confirm the $\Theta(1/\sqrt{n})$ scaling.
why_interesting: The study of localization transitions driven by nonlinearity is central to understanding nonlinear wave phenomena in physical systems, which has parallels with Anderson localization and energy band structure analysis relevant to quantum dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic of the ﬁnite FPUT-type diatomic chain (1) of length 2n. The masses are identical; the straight single and double lines denote the linear interior springs k1, k2, the springs at the two ends denote the clamped boundary springs k3,1, k3,2 to ﬁxed walls, and the curved springs denote the cubic FPUT nonlinearity, which acts on the relative displacement of each pair of connected sites.

Figure 2: Regime (R1) (middle-localized), 2n = 200, k1 = 1, k2 = 2.3, k3,1 = 1.3, k3,2 = 3.5. (a) The branch frequency ω (solid) and its leading approximation (30) (dash–dotted) as functions of the energy E, drift downward from the lower optical edge √2k2 (dashed horizontal). (b) The normalized periodic orbit Q(0) at small (blue) and large (red) amplitude: the uniformly extended near-edge mode evolves into a state concentrated in the middle of the chain.

Figure 3: Regime (R2) (edge localization), 2n = 200, k1 = 1, k2 = 2.3, k3,1 = 1.3, k3,2 = 4.6 = 2k2. (a) The branch frequency ω (solid) and its leading approximation (40) (dash–dotted) drift down from the lower optical edge (dashed horizontal). (b) The normalized periodic orbit Q(0) at small (blue) and large (red) amplitude: the uniformly extended mode evolves into a state concentrated at the right boundary.
